questions.question: What is the main goal of Positive Behaviour Support?
options: To improve the person's quality of life, so the needs behind their behaviour are met
To manage the person so that staff have an easier shift
To stop the behaviour as quickly as possible using firm rules
To punish behaviour that others find difficult

questions.question: In PBS, what does it mean that support is 'person-centred'?
options: Support is decided by whatever is easiest for staff
Support focuses mainly on stopping the behaviour
Support is built around the individual rather than around control
Support is the same standard approach for everyone

questions.question: What is the care worker's role in Positive Behaviour Support?
options: To design the person's PBS plan and carry out a functional assessment
To manage the person so their behaviour is kept under control
To decide when restrictive practices should become routine
To follow the person's PBS plan and work in the least restrictive way

questions.question: What does this Positive Behaviour Support e-learning course cover?
options: Full accredited training in physical intervention
The knowledge and awareness element only
How to carry out a functional assessment independently
How to write a person's PBS plan on your own

questions.question: Who develops and leads PBS plans, including any functional assessment?
options: Trained professionals
Whichever staff member is most senior that day
Any care worker who is on shift
The person's family on their own

questions.question: What is required before anyone uses physical intervention?
options: Reading the person's PBS plan is enough on its own
A quick briefing at handover
Specific, accredited training from a recognised programme
Watching a colleague carry it out once

questions.question: Why does PBS avoid talking about 'managing' people?
options: Because people are not problems to be managed; they should be understood and supported
Because 'managing' is too difficult a word for staff to use
Because the behaviour should simply be ignored
Because managing people is only the job of senior staff

questions.question: When a person's behaviour is difficult, which question does PBS ask?
options: How do we make sure this never inconveniences staff?
How do we stop this behaviour as fast as possible?
What consequence will teach them not to do it again?
What is this person trying to tell us, and what do they need?

questions.question: How did older, control-based approaches typically respond to behaviour that challenged?
options: By improving the person's quality of life
By controlling the behaviour, often through punishment or restriction
By changing the service to fit the person
By understanding the need behind the behaviour

questions.question: Which statement reflects the values base of PBS?
options: The service changes to fit the person
Dignity and choice are optional extras
Some people simply have to be controlled for their own good
The person must change to fit the service

questions.question: What does it mean to say that all behaviour has a function?
options: Behaviour is simply part of the person's condition
Behaviour has a purpose or reason for the person, even when it is not obvious
Behaviour is always done deliberately to upset staff
Behaviour is random and has no meaning

questions.question: In PBS, behaviour that others find challenging is very often best understood as...
options: the communication of an unmet need or of distress
something that should always be ignored
a sign the person is behaving badly on purpose
proof that the person cannot be helped

questions.question: For which groups is behaviour especially likely to be a form of communication?
options: Some people with a learning disability, autistic people, and people living with advanced dementia
Only people who are physically unwell
Only people who choose not to speak
Only young children

questions.question: Which of these is an example of a behaviour serving the function of gaining something?
options: Shouting so that a staff member comes to sit with them for company
Pushing away a task to get out of a demand
Rocking to meet a sensory need
Covering the ears to reduce a loud noise

questions.question: A person shouts and pushes their plate away to get out of a noisy, crowded dining room. What is the likely function of this behaviour?
options: To meet a sensory need
To gain attention from the other residents
To gain a favourite item
To avoid or escape something that overwhelms them

questions.question: A person with a sore tooth and no words to say so begins shouting at mealtimes. This behaviour is most likely...
options: an expression of pain or distress
a sign they dislike the other residents
a deliberate attempt to annoy staff
an attempt to gain a treat

questions.question: How can a care worker best help trained professionals work out the function of a behaviour?
options: By carefully observing and recording what happened before and after the behaviour
By ignoring the behaviour completely
By giving the person whatever stops the behaviour in the moment
By deciding the function themselves and acting on it

questions.question: Which of these is an example of an immediate trigger for distress?
options: A poor night's sleep the day before
A sudden change of plan or being rushed
Having plenty of meaningful activity
A calm, familiar routine

questions.question: Which of these is best described as a 'setting event' that makes distress more likely later?
options: An upsetting phone call earlier in the day
Being offered a genuine choice
A sudden loud noise in the moment
A predictable daily routine

questions.question: Why does knowing a person's history and how they communicate matter in PBS?
options: It lets you decide which behaviours to punish
It helps you spot early signs of distress and avoid known triggers
It lets you predict everyone's behaviour so the plan is not needed
It is only useful for keeping records

questions.question: What is 'primary prevention' in PBS?
options: The physical holds used as a last resort
Getting everyday things right so needs are met and distress is far less likely to build
The paperwork completed after an incident
The reactive strategies used in a crisis

questions.question: Which best describes a good environment under PBS?
options: Calm surroundings suited to the person, with their familiar belongings around them
As busy, noisy and stimulating as possible
Bare and empty so that nothing can be broken
Kept identical for every person regardless of preference

questions.question: Why is meaningful activity important in PBS?
options: It gives the person a reason to get up and prevents long empty hours
It removes the need for a PBS plan
It is a way of controlling the person's behaviour
It keeps staff busy

questions.question: What are secondary strategies in PBS?
options: Things you do at the early stage of distress to help someone calm down before it grows
The proactive support that fills a person's whole day
The records completed after an incident
Physical holds used when someone is at serious risk of harm

questions.question: Under least restrictive practice, restriction should be used...
options: only to keep someone safe, limiting their freedom as little as possible and for as short a time as possible
routinely, so that staff can keep control
whenever it is more convenient for staff
as a punishment when someone behaves badly

questions.question: PBS focuses on improving the person's quality of life rather than simply stopping their behaviour.
options: True
False

questions.question: In PBS, the right approach is to 'manage' people and their behaviour.
options: True
False

questions.question: All behaviour has a function, even when the reason is not obvious to us.
options: True
False

questions.question: Behaviour is best understood as simply part of a person's condition that cannot change.
options: True
False

questions.question: In PBS, the whole team should follow the same agreed plan consistently.
options: True
False

questions.question: Physical intervention can be safely learned from an online awareness course like this one.
options: True
False

questions.question: In PBS, reactive strategies are used routinely as a first response to behaviour.
options: True
False

questions.question: Every use of a restrictive practice must be recorded and reviewed.
options: True
False

questions.question: Which of the following are part of proactive, primary-prevention support in PBS? (Select all that apply)
options: Routine physical restraint
A good, calm environment that suits the person
Predictable, reassuring routines
Meaningful activity the person enjoys and values
Punishing behaviour that others find difficult

questions.question: Which of the following can act as triggers or setting events for a person's distress? (Select all that apply)
options: A sudden loud noise
Being well rested and calm
A poor night's sleep the night before
Being hungry, tired or in pain
Having plenty of genuine choice

questions.question: Which of the following are early warning signs that a person may be becoming anxious or unhappy? (Select all that apply)
options: Pacing or fidgeting
Going quiet
A change in facial expression
Eating a good meal calmly
Laughing happily with friends

questions.question: Which of the following count as restrictive practices? (Select all that apply)
options: Seclusion
Supporting the person to do a meaningful activity
Using medication to control behaviour
Physical restraint
Offering the person a genuine choice

questions.question: What should happen after an incident in PBS? (Select all that apply)
options: Provide post-incident support to the person and to staff
Reflect and learn what triggered the incident and what would help next time
Ignore the incident so no one dwells on it
Use restriction afterwards as a punishment
Record clearly and factually what happened, including any restriction used

questions.question: Which of the following are communication methods that may suit a person you support? (Select all that apply)
options: Pictures or objects
Long, complex sentences with no time to respond
Simple language
Signing or a communication aid
Refusing to check you have understood them

questions.question: When a person is starting to become distressed, which of the following are helpful secondary strategies? (Select all that apply)
options: Staying calm and speaking softly
Following the specific strategies in the person's PBS plan
Reducing whatever is upsetting them, such as noise or demands
Insisting they do as they are told straight away
Raising your voice so they take you seriously

questions.question: In PBS, behaviour is understood as a form of ___ of an unmet need.
The Mental Capacity Act requires that a decision made for someone who cannot make it themselves must be in their best interests and the least ___ of their rights and freedoms.
Getting everyday things right so that a person's needs are met and distress is far less likely to build is called ___ prevention.
___ strategies are what the team does at the early stage, when a person becomes anxious, to help them calm down before their distress grows.
In PBS, reactive strategies are always a ___ resort, used as little as possible.
